verdicts.note: The sole citation is a single remark that Blake's outcome 'astonishes average citizens and undermines trust' — a court-legitimacy critique already doing full duty for his judicial -2, not a bail/diversion position. He has no criminal-law professional formation at all (tax/securities/FINRA). Deriving an enforcement-side safety posture from this one ambiguous comment is an indirect inference; confidence belongs at low (excluded from alignment math), and medium overstated it.

The basis concedes there is no stated interpretive method; the -1 is reverse-engineered from policy-outcome pledges (opposes a state income tax, 'won't be soft on criminals'). Outcome commitments are not an as-written philosophy — prejudging results is the opposite of pole_a's restraint/depoliticization — and mapping anti-tax/tough-on-crime onto the textualist pole is partisan inference in judicial-philosophy clothing. The dossier itself flags his statements as unusual precisely because they are not philosophy. Omit.

missing.basis: His pamphlet pledge that he 'won't be soft on criminals' is a direct, documented enforcement-side posture toward criminal defendants — far more probative for the safety axis than for judicial philosophy, where the scorer placed it. Single clear statement supports medium; flagged for the merger because it is a stated posture rather than the bail/diversion/formation evidence the guide enumerates.

review_notes: Stephens judicial +1/high is the best-supported philosophy score in the judicial races: verified McCleary authorship (state constitution as positive-rights guarantor) tempered by her law-not-politics platform; -not- inferred from endorsements. Merchant's three scores are internally consistent and evidence-rich: explicit as-written method applied even where it favors defendants (supports Blake), 15-year public-defense formation for safety +1, and a by-design outsider profile. Bloom judicial -2/medium holds on repeated restraint statements (loyalty to the Constitution, judges should not make law, criticism of both Quinn and Blake) independent of the WAGOP recommendation.
